summary: Yegge argues that the future of coding belongs not to individual coding agents but to orchestrated colonies of agents working together, as exemplified by his tool Gas Town. He traces Gas Town through four prior iterations and explains why it will improve automatically through smarter models, community contributions, training corpus inclusion, and agent vendors adapting to support colony-style work. He predicts big companies will be disrupted because small teams using agent swarms achieve velocities that larger organizations simply cannot coordinate around. The post also covers his preference for Go in vibe-coded projects and warns that developers who haven't adopted coding agents are falling behind.
key_points: The future of coding agents is colonies and factories, not bigger individual agents — nature prefers ant colonies over giant ants
Gas Town will improve passively through smarter models, community growth, training corpus inclusion, and agent vendors adding orchestration APIs
Go is surprisingly well-suited for AI-assisted coding because its boring simplicity means diffs are always readable, unlike TypeScript's verbose type gymnastics
Big companies face an existential threat because small teams with agent swarms move so fast that coordination across large orgs becomes nearly impossible
Coding agent vendors need to shift from building 'human pair programmers' to building 'colony workers' with proper automation APIs
money_quotes: When work needs to be done, nature prefers colonies.
Claude Code is 'the world's biggest fuckin' ant.' It will bite you in half and take all your resources.
Colonies are going to win. Factories are going to win. Automation is going to win. Of course they're gonna fucking win.
The entire world is going to explode into tiny companies, which will then aggregate and re-form into larger ones.
When the diffs go by in TypeScript, half the time you're like, what awful thing is my computer up to now? But with Go, it's just boring.
At this rate I'll be more impressed if a 100-person startup can make a billion dollars in 2027.
Programming has always been a best-effort, we'll-fix-shit-later endeavor.
key_insight: The competitive advantage in AI-assisted coding will shift from making individual agents smarter to orchestrating colonies of agents into automated factories, fundamentally disrupting how companies are sized and organized.
